text: David mustered the troops who were with him and appointed commanders over thousands and over hundreds.
He sent the army out in three divisions, one under Joab, one under Joab's brother Abishai, and one under Ittai the Gittite, and told the troops he intended to go out with them himself.

But the troops said he should not go, because if they had to flee, no one would care, and even if half of them died, no one would care — but he was worth ten thousand of them, and it would be better for him to be ready to send help from the city.
The king agreed to do whatever seemed best to them, and stood beside the gate while the whole army marched out by hundreds and thousands.
The king ordered Joab, Abishai, and Ittai, in front of everyone, to deal gently for his sake with the young man Absalom.
The army went out into the countryside to meet Israel, and the battle took place in the forest of Ephraim.
Israel's forces were defeated there by David's men, and the losses that day were heavy — twenty thousand men.
The fighting spread out across the whole region, and the forest itself claimed more lives that day than the actual fighting did.

Absalom happened to run into some of David's men while riding his mule; the mule went under the thick branches of a large oak, and Absalom's head got caught fast in the tree, leaving him hanging between heaven and earth while the mule kept going without him.
A man who saw this told Joab that he had seen Absalom hanging in an oak tree.
Joab asked the man why he hadn't struck Absalom down right there, saying he would have rewarded him with ten pieces of silver and a belt.

The man answered that even for a thousand pieces of silver he would not have raised a hand against the king's son, since they had all heard the king order Joab, Abishai, and Ittai to protect the young man Absalom for his sake.
He added that if he had acted against Absalom, nothing would have stayed hidden from the king, and Joab would have been the first to distance himself from him.
Joab said he wasn't going to waste more time talking, took three javelins, and drove them into Absalom's chest while he was still alive in the tree.
Ten of Joab's young armor-bearers then surrounded Absalom, struck him, and killed him.
Joab sounded the trumpet to call the troops back from chasing after Israel, since he had them stop the pursuit.
They took Absalom's body, threw it into a large pit in the forest, and piled a huge mound of stones over it, while the rest of Israel fled to their homes.
Absalom had earlier set up a pillar for himself in the King's Valley, since he had no son to keep his name alive, and had named it after himself; it was still known as Absalom's monument.
Ahimaaz son of Zadok asked to run and tell the king that the LORD had delivered him from his enemies.
Joab told him not to be the one carrying news today, since the king's son was dead, though he could carry news another time.
Instead Joab told the Cushite to go report what he had seen, and the Cushite bowed to Joab and ran off.
Ahimaaz asked Joab again to let him run too, whatever happened, and Joab asked why he wanted to, since there would be no reward waiting for him.
Ahimaaz insisted on running anyway, so Joab let him go, and he ran by way of the plain and got ahead of the Cushite.
David was sitting between the two gates, and a watchman went up to the roof over the gate and saw a man running alone.
The watchman called down to report it, and the king said that if the man was alone, he likely carried news; the runner kept getting closer.
Then the watchman spotted another man running and called out that a second runner was alone as well, and the king said he too must be bringing news.
The watchman said the first runner's stride looked like Ahimaaz son of Zadok, and the king said he was a good man who would bring good news.
Ahimaaz called out to the king that all was well, bowed low before him, and said blessed be the LORD who had handed over the men who had rebelled against the king.
The king asked if the young man Absalom was safe, and Ahimaaz said that when Joab sent him he had seen a great commotion but didn't know exactly what had happened.
The king told him to step aside and wait there, so he moved aside and stood.
Then the Cushite arrived and announced good news for the king, since the LORD had delivered him that day from all who had risen against him.
The king asked the Cushite the same question about Absalom's safety, and the Cushite answered that may the king's enemies end up like that young man had.
The king was overcome with grief, went up to the room above the gate, and wept, crying out that he wished he had died instead of his son, Absalom, his son, his son.
